reviews.review_text:
I have never seen another game capable of invoking the same feelings MORDHAU does to the same degree, and I'm not sure I ever will. There's a degree of connection between controls and character movement that lands somewhere between traditional fighting games and VR, and which lends itself to a uniquely dynamic, expressive link between player mentality and behaviour.

Which is important, because MORDHAU is a game about predictive analysis as much as it is about reaction time and raw technical skill. You can brainlessly swing a War Axe through team fights, and your score won't hurt, but there's a lot of depth to be found if you decide to dip your toes into learning the combat system thoroughly. Universally low time-to-kill (TTK) results in a system where competence dictates how much of your success is dictated by strategy, and how much is left to luck. You can't trick an experienced duelist the same way you can a new player, because they don't have the same expectations; as a result, you have to read and understand each opponent as they come, or else leave the outcome to pure chance.

With that out of the way, I'll zoom out a little bit.

You can engage with the game to any degree you like, really. Owing to the game's relatively small community and extreme range of expression (via body language, wonderfully silly voice-lines, and emotes), there's a subculture that reflects this openness that most players don't really tap into until they've gotten several hundred hours in. 

In any given match, you could find a fortified shield wall, a jester-garbed bards playing to a crowd, an orderly line of enemies and allies waiting for their turn at the ballista, a siege-ready catapult-trampoline, a horde of cavemen, or almsot anything else. You might join your team on one final, dramatic push to claim the objective—or you might sneak past enemy lines to play the saboteur. Hell, you might run around throwing out med-bags for friends and foes alike, or hang out in the enemy commander's company while enduring Katy Perry's entire discography on lute. Or maybe you'll be hard at work in the duelyard, learning from passionate players and the most annoying ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ you've ever met alike. The list goes on, and on: there's a lot to do in this playground, and it's pretty much always by your whim.

On a gameplay level, I have plenty of smaller criticisms, ranging from the balance of entertainment value of particular maps to the cost-weight of builds or weapons. The tutorial sucks, and efforts towards player retention are lacklustre. But I only care about that stuff because I'm a nerd who's spent way too much time on this game, and will probably spend a lot more. Ultimately, the early game will be an exercise in pain tolerance that either pushes you away or draws you in. And once you're in, there's really nothing quite like it.

also, fashionhau. this game's customization clears just about any other's and it's not even close. i will not be taking opinions on this matter

Hundreds of reviews detailing this situation have been deleted from this store page. One of the worst cases of scamming on steam I've ever seen. One in three players in this game are using one or all of these cheats, there is  no way to votekick them, and there is no way to report them. Auto-Parry: Automatically blocks incoming attacks from any direction, making the player nearly impossible to hit.
Speedhacks: Increases movement or swing speed, often subtly (+5%) to avoid detection.
Aim-Bots: primarily used for projectile weapons like bows or javelins to ensure perfect accuracy.
Instant Swings: Eliminates weapon cooldowns or "turncaps," allowing for near-instantaneous attacks.
Subtle Manipulation: Minor adjustments to swing animations or reach that are difficult to distinguish from high-level skill. The game's moderators accept kickback payments from the sale of cheat programs. The perpetrators of this scam will claim such things don't exist, two seconds searching google reveals that this is an absurd lie. The situation has gotten so bad the game is unplayable. This is what killed Mordhau. Meanwhile Triternion gets to continue to scam steam users, and Steam continues to remove reviews that expose this total exploitation of their userbase. How this game is not delisted is baffling.
The game has good mechanics, the problem is the players. most are sweaty af and pieces of human garbage the world would be better off without.

Horribly toxic elitist community is all that remains in this shell of a game. Save your time and money for literlly anything else.

Or join and have fun getting team killed for picking the "wrong" class.

Great game super fun, but the playerbase is only like 5 people that are super sweaty and the people that dont have 4,000 hours on the game either third party on the others or have a horrible K/D ratio but when its just your friends in a free for all its one of the funnest games i have ever played.

As much as I hate to say it, the game is dead.
I'm unable to connect to the servers agains. I had a lot of good memories with this game, I only wish I'd known the servers would die so that I may play it one last time.

Unfortunately can't recommend for new players. Don't buy unless it's on sale.

Pros:
- One of the best melee combat systems and unique mechanics i've experienced in games overall. Easy to learn hard to master type.
- Decent character customization. Alot of well designed equipment, room for fashion is wild. Variable and fun to use weapons.
- I don't get why people claim comunity is toxic or something. Not exactly very noob-friendly, yes, but expirience i had was fun as hell. Everybody's constantly screaming halerious warcries all over the place, spamming gestures and playing on lute (tbh i wasn't actually looking in chat much).
 - Well made maps. Some better, some worse, not very much, like 5-6 actually playable, but overall they're pretty cool in terms of multiple paths, enviromental design, traps and team balance.

Cons:
- The game is basically dead. Peak online less then 1k. You will constantly bump into same people. Searching 10+ mins for a 20 min match on Europe server at 12 p.m. for multiplayer game it's a death sentence.
- If you're new player and trying to learn how to play, you're kinda screwed. First 20 matches i had KDA something like 4/25 and thought i'm just bad and doing something wrong. But i was not. And then i realised each time i was killed, it was by the same guy with KDA 24/3 and 200 rank, while i was 6. Considering how ranking works, that dude spend something around 500 hours on this game, maybe more. I saw very little players with levels like mine or near it. Simple ranked matchmaking system would easily solve the problem, but it's already too late. The only ranking exists in 1v1 mode and guess what - of course, it's occupied with dudes with 300+ hrs and smurfs. There's no way to learn game mechanics except 5min tutorial and stupid like a cabbages bots. I can parry 20 hits from bot in a row, but what the point? Yeah, some may say 'skill issue'. Ok, but how i supposed to learn game to git gud when i constantly dying in 1-2 hits or being ganked by 3+ people? It's just not fun nor fair, why even bother then? No wonder why there's no new players, huh.
- Unfit and absolutely unnecessary system for getting new stuff. You need both in-game currency and experience points, you get in regular matches, to buy accessories and weapons. In already paid game. With no microtransactions. Why????? If it was a FtP game, maybe it would be reasonable as alternative for paid cosmetics. As a result there's always 3 to 5 dudes in match just standing afk or playing lute on base and farming points while everyone else is fighting. And i would understand if it was only for fashion, but you also need to unlock basic weapons aside of just skins.
- Most of game modes are boring and abandoned. Noone playing anything exept a frontline 48x48 (usually it's more like 10x10) or 1v1. No PvE mode also (Horde is just awful, tried playing it only once though). If you want actually have fun with this game you need atleast  3-4 friends to play with or you'll just give up and leave game after 20+ hrs (unless you already spent 2000 hrs in it and forget what fresh air is).
- Some balance issues. Some weapons are just useless (e.g. daggers or mallets) while others is little too overpowered (maul one-shotting in head in any armor, spears are annoying). Almost nobody playing as engineer or archer. Completing team goals like capturing a zone or drag stones to load the cart have no gameplay sense or motivation to do them - everyone is just fighting in the middle of the field, brainlessly, crowd on crowd; winning or losing a match does nothing as same. Considering developers haven't updated their game for 2 years, unlikely something will change in the future.

I really want to like this gaem. If only I could play it in 2020. It's jsut sad...

First time playing, loaded into a lobby full of Russians duelling. Proceeded to get absolutely destroyed. Tried again, same thing. Suddenly 10 Russians tea bagging my corpse and stun locking me in a circle with punches. Few hours later I come back freshly trained by one Russian who kept duelling me non stop. I get destroyed again, but at least this time there's a bard playing It's Not Unusual by Tom Jones on the lute. 

12/10 game, incredibly competitive, but it sure is fun.

What an awsome game! 

Combat is fast paced and visceral, with plenty of depth and you have myriad weapons to choose from. While duels and skirmishes are fun, the atmosphere in the large-scale battles is really something special. Chaotic as they are, they truely feel as if you are on a medieval battlefield. 

Unfortunately, this game is breathing it's last. I returned after some time away to find the playerbase largely vanished. There are only a few hundred players online at the best of times, and the handfull of servers worth joining are often dominated by sweatlords with thousands of hours in the game. 

Still, the game is usually a lot of fun, and is worth checking out if you have any interest in medieval combat games!
